meta_project.persona_name: Deadline Deepak, the time-starved tech professional
meta_project.tone_source: Derived from JTBD interviews of metro IT/tech professionals who order delivery 12-25x/month and already pay for both Swiggy One and a health app; voice is pragmatic, wry, and time-protective.

voice_samples.professional: I don't want another app to manage — I want one less decision in my day and a bill I can actually predict.
voice_samples.authoritative: I already order in every day. Don't teach me to cook; just make the ordering smart and stop it bleeding money.
voice_samples.confident: If it hits my protein and stays under my cap, I'll tap confirm at noon and never think about lunch again.

emotional_drivers.primary: Relief — reclaiming the daily decision energy that sprints and calls already drain.
emotional_drivers.secondary: Control — a food spend and a diet that finally stay inside the lines he set.
emotional_drivers.tertiary: Self-respect — eating like an adult who has his life handled, without the effort.

demographics.company_type: Metro tech hubs — Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Pune, Gurgaon, Chennai; 26-35; lives alone or with flatmates; no cook

pain_points: Loses 15 minutes and his 4pm decision energy to 'what do I eat' every single day.
Defaults to the same three unhealthy restaurants and impulse-orders at peak hunger and peak price.
Monthly delivery bill balloons to ₹8k-15k he cannot account for.
Diet collapses midweek because no delivery flow shows a dish's macros.
goals: Never think about routine meals again, without spending more.
Hit protein and calorie targets on autopilot.
Keep monthly food spend under a hard, self-set cap.
objections: I don't want a system spending my money without me looking.
Auto-plans will feel repetitive and robotic by week three.
Can the macro numbers on restaurant food actually be trusted?
